title: The War May Be Ending. What Did Epic Fury Cost?
gist: CSIS estimates the U.S. 2026 Operation Epic Fury against Iran cost roughly $40 billion ($34–42B), dominated by $26.1B in expensive missiles, with neither the FY2026 nor FY2027 budget covering it — setting up a 'bruising' congressional funding battle over a supplemental versus reprogramming, even as the shooting ends.

insights.claim: CSIS estimates Operation Epic Fury cost the DOD around $40 billion (range $34–42B) across six categories: deployment, munitions, higher op-tempo, equipment losses, base damage, and higher fuel prices.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Frames the fiscal hole Congress must fill — the unbudgeted ~$40B is the catalyst for the coming appropriations fight.
insights.quote: CSIS estimates war costs to be around $40 billion ($34 billion to $42 billion).
insights.locator: intro

insights.claim: Neither the FY2026 DOD budget nor the president's FY2027 budget proposal included war costs, leaving the entire ~$40B uncovered.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The funding gap is structural, not accounting — it forces new congressional action rather than internal reallocation.
insights.quote: neither the FY 2026 Department of Defense (DOD) budget nor the president’s FY 2027 budget proposal include war costs
insights.locator: intro

insights.claim: War costs came in four phases — a multi-week deployment, ~5 days of expensive missile use, a third phase where Iranian attacks fell 80–90 percent and the coalition shifted to cheap short-range munitions, then declining ceasefire costs that never hit zero.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The non-linear cost curve explains why per-day estimates from week one massively overstate the total — a key flaw in rival trackers.
insights.locator: intro

insights.claim: DOD first cited $25B then $29B in war costs but admitted those excluded base damage — the major driver of CSIS's higher figure.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Base damage is the contested gap between official and independent estimates — and the line item DOD has political reasons to omit.
insights.quote: Defense officials stated that their estimate does not include base damage
insights.locator: How This Estimate Compares to Others

insights.claim: The Iran War Cost Tracker's $113B figure simply extrapolates a $1B/day week-one DOD estimate, ignoring post-ceasefire strike reductions and the shift to cheaper munitions.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: High-profile headline war-cost numbers are methodologically inflated — useful framing for skeptically reading sensational estimates.
insights.quote: This approach does not reflect the reduction in U.S. strikes after the ceasefire or the use of cheaper munitions
insights.locator: How This Estimate Compares to Others

insights.claim: The reported DOD wartime supplemental request of $80 billion is roughly double CSIS's estimate because it bundles in non-war items such as 'jump-starting' the munitions acquisition process.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The $80B ask is a vehicle for broader defense-industrial spending — watch what rides on the supplemental beyond actual war costs.
insights.quote: the reported DOD wartime supplemental request of $80 billion
insights.locator: How This Estimate Compares to Others

insights.claim: Munitions at $26.1 billion are by far the largest war-cost component, and all expended munitions need replacement since prewar inventories were already considered inadequate.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: A $26B replenishment wave is a direct demand signal for missile primes — Tomahawk, JASSM, PrSM/ATACMS makers.
insights.quote: Munitions: $26.1 billion. U.S. forces fired many expensive missiles, making munitions the largest component
insights.locator: Costs for the Department of Defense

insights.claim: U.S. forces fired 13,629 strike munitions against more than 13,000 targets by the ceasefire, per CENTCOM commander Admiral Brad Cooper, plus skirmishing including 49 Tomahawks on June 10.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The raw expenditure volume quantifies the inventory drawdown driving multi-year replacement procurement.
insights.quote: U.S. forces fired 13,629 strike munitions against more than 13,000 targets by the time of the ceasefire
insights.locator: Costs for the Department of Defense

insights.claim: Operational success collapsed daily munitions cost: after a few days the U.S. shifted to cheap JDAM-kitted dumb bombs (~$100,000) over Tomahawks (~$2.6 million) for the same 1,000-lb precision payload.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Air superiority is the lever that drops cost-per-target ~26x — the single biggest factor compressing total war cost.
insights.quote: A single dumb bomb fitted with a JDAM guidance kit and a Tomahawk both deliver 1,000 lb payloads with extreme precision but at a much different cost ($2.6 million versus $100,000).
insights.locator: Costs for the Department of Defense

insights.claim: Low-cost attack-drone alternatives such as LUCAS, a U.S. copy of the Iranian Shahed-136, are not yet in serial production, forcing reliance on expensive long-range munitions early in the war.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The absence of serial cheap-drone production is a U.S. capability gap — a procurement priority and investment theme.
insights.quote: Low-cost alternatives such as LUCAS, a copy of the Iranian Shahed 136, are not yet in serial production.
insights.locator: Costs for the Department of Defense

insights.claim: Air-defense cost assumed the U.S. shot down 40–60 percent of incoming drones/missiles under a 'shoot-look-shoot' doctrine, with coalition partners intercepting the rest and sharply cutting U.S. costs and losses.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Coalition burden-sharing materially lowered U.S. interceptor consumption — alliance value is quantifiable in cost terms.
insights.quote: Coalition partners greatly decreased U.S. costs and losses by intercepting rest of the drones
insights.locator: Costs for the Department of Defense

insights.claim: Equipment losses of $1.8–3.5B covered 42 lost or damaged aircraft (per CRS) plus tilt-rotors and a THAAD radar, with most losses being uncrewed drones the U.S. is more willing to risk.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The skew toward expendable drones over crewed jets reflects a deliberate risk posture — and lighter replacement-cost pressure than headline aircraft losses suggest.
insights.quote: most lost aircraft are uncrewed drones. The United States is much more willing to risk uncrewed aircraft than crewed aircraft.
insights.locator: Equipment Losses and Damages

insights.claim: Base and fixed-infrastructure damage is estimated at $4.0–9.4 billion, with the wide range driven by uncertainty over how many structures were struck — CSIS examined 122, the Washington Post identified 228.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Base damage is the most uncertain and most contested cost line — and the one DOD excluded, making it the swing factor in total estimates.
insights.quote: CSIS analysis produced a range of $2.2–$5.1 billion for the 122 structures examined
insights.locator: Damage to Bases and Fixed Infrastructure

insights.claim: Iranian strikes on U.S. bases were precise with no random craters, hitting not just warfighting facilities but barracks, gyms and dining halls — indicating an intent to cause casualties — and Iran may have had Russian or Chinese intelligence help on mobile targets.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Precision targeting of personnel facilities plus possible great-power intel support is an escalation signal worth tracking for future conflicts.
insights.quote: The images make clear the Iranian strikes were precise. There are no random craters indicating misses or wasted assets.
insights.locator: Damage to Bases and Fixed Infrastructure

insights.claim: Despite roughly 10 percent leakage past coalition interceptors causing significant base damage, there is little evidence Iranian attacks materially restricted U.S. operations during the campaign.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Damage was costly but not operationally decisive — relevant to assessing the real military efficacy of Iran's missile/drone arsenal.
insights.quote: there is little evidence that Iranian attacks materially restricted U.S. operations during this campaign
insights.locator: Damage to Bases and Fixed Infrastructure

insights.claim: Higher fuel prices cost the DOD $1.4 billion as jet fuel rose ~$0.79/gallon above the budgeted $3.67 against projected use of 3.05 billion gallons in 2026.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Even modest fuel-price moves carry large absolute costs for the DOD's enormous consumption — an energy-price transmission channel into the defense budget.
insights.quote: current prices, which rose to about $4.46/gallon—$0.79 above what the DOD budgeted
insights.locator: Other DOD Costs

insights.claim: Non-DOD federal costs total about $1 billion across Homeland Security, State, Veterans Affairs, and Energy, including NNSA costs for monitoring and disposing of Iran's nuclear materials.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: War costs ripple beyond the Pentagon — nuclear-material disposal signals an active dismantling track on Iran's program.
insights.locator: Costs for the Federal Government Outside the DOD

insights.claim: Future veterans' costs run ~$400 million annually and ~$12 billion over 30 years — far below Iraq/Afghanistan because only ~70,000 service members were involved (vs. 2 million) over four months (vs. 20 years).
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: A large but deferred liability with no accrual funding — a hidden long-tail cost the current budget cannot capture.
insights.quote: The total cost over 30 years ($12 billion) is large but much less than that for veterans of the Iraq and Afghanistan wars
insights.locator: Costs for the Federal Government Outside the DOD

insights.claim: Beyond the federal budget, the Cost of War project estimated $40 billion in added fuel costs to the U.S. public and Moody's estimated the war will cost Americans $132 billion.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Economy-wide costs dwarf the DOD bill — the energy-price channel is where the war hit ordinary Americans hardest.
insights.quote: Moody’s estimated that the war will cost Americans $132 billion
insights.locator: Economic and Human Costs

insights.claim: Human costs were highly asymmetric: 13 U.S. casualties, ~40 Israeli, at least 30 in the Gulf States, but a reported 1,700 Iranian civilian fatalities.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: The casualty asymmetry shapes domestic political tolerance — low U.S. losses ease the funding fight but the war's human toll fuels antiwar sentiment.
insights.quote: Iran is reported to have suffered 1,700 civilian fatalities
insights.locator: Economic and Human Costs

insights.claim: Funding options are a supplemental appropriation (DOD's preference), amending the FY2027 request, or reprogramming from the $153B in the FY2026 reconciliation bill — but the latter lacks transfer authority and requires new legislation.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: Each path requires active congressional action, not acquiescence — funding mechanism choice signals winners/losers among defense programs.
insights.quote: the legislation did not provide transfer authority to move that money around
insights.locator: Now What?

insights.claim: Cancian predicts a 'bruising' congressional battle to fund the war even if successful, as it becomes a focus for antiwar sentiment including among Republicans, with DOD warning of an operating-funds shortage by late summer.
insights.speaker: Mark F. Cancian
insights.so_what: A near-term funding cliff plus political friction creates defense-appropriations uncertainty — a watch item for defense-budget exposure.
insights.quote: The shooting war has ended, but the budget war has just begun.
insights.locator: Now What?
